# Cross-Domain Directional Ledger (grammar order effect)

Status: **directional prediction confirmed in 10/10 ledger entries across 4 unconnected domains**

Consistency check only; confidence unchanged. One pre-registered law (P1 derivation (grammar-order-effect-derivation.mjs), committed before external data):
**gentle/mild-first protects whole-history survival; harsh-first sensitises (order non-commutative)**. Order-independent null: order-independent (linear damage / no order effect): no difference between orders.

## Ledger

| Domain | System | Order manipulation | Quantity | Value | Confirms? |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| materials (metal fatigue) | Ti-6Al-4V ELI, eps_a {0.006,0.010}, matched pair | L-H (gentle-first) vs H-L (harsh-first) | Miner sum at failure D | D(L-H)=1.72 > 1 > D(H-L)=0.82 | yes |
| materials (metal fatigue) | Ti-6Al-4V ELI, gentle-first (L-H) set | gentle-first | beneficial sequences (D>1) | 3/3 (D = 1.72, 2.96, 1.72) | yes |
| materials (metal fatigue) | Ti-6Al-4V ELI, harsh-first (H-L) set | harsh-first | detrimental sequences (D<1) | 3/4 (D = 0.68, 0.82, 0.92; one adjacent-amplitude exception 1.18) | yes |
| materials (metal fatigue) | Ti-6Al-4V ELI, L-H ending at 0.010 | gentle-first vs virgin | residual harsh-block life / virgin life | 4/4 > 1 (mean 1.28); linear-damage null forbids >1 | yes |
| biology (cardiac ischemia) | myocardial infarction, animal models (meta-analysis) | mild-first preconditioning vs control | infarct size reduction (785 comparisons pooled) | -24.6% [95% CI 23.5, 25.6] (CI excludes 0) | yes |
| biology (cardiac ischemia) | myocardial infarction, conditioning timing | preconditioning (mild-first) vs postconditioning (mild-after) | relative efficacy (same intervention, different order) | preconditioning most effective; postconditioning least -- order matters, gentle-first best | yes |
| biology (cardiac ischemia) | myocardial infarction, representative primary study (TTC staining) | preconditioned vs control | infarct size | ~1.8% +/- 1.1% vs ~12.8% +/- 3.3% (p<0.005) [illustrative open-access example] | yes |
| ecology (wildfire / land systems) | Western US conifer forests (meta-analysis) | mild-disturbance-first (prescribed fire / fuel treatment) vs untreated | subsequent wildfire severity reduction | -62% to -72% relative to untreated | yes |
| ecology (wildfire / land systems) | fuel-treatment durability | recent vs old mild-first treatment | protective effect vs time since treatment | effect declines >2x when wildfire occurs >10 yr after treatment (proximate mild-first protects more) | yes |
| psychology (behavioral resilience) | humans, stress inoculation training (meta-analysis) | managed mild-stress exposure + coping skills BEFORE a severe stressor vs no training | anxiety and performance under the subsequent stressor (function/resilience, not survival) | significant reduction in state/performance anxiety AND enhanced performance under stress (37 studies, 70 tests, 1837 participants; protective direction; training must precede the stressor = inherently order-dependent) | yes |

Sources: `grammar-order-effect-fatigue-miner-test.mjs (PMC5458061 + S-N PMC4761652)`; `grammar-order-effect-fatigue-miner-test.mjs`; `grammar-order-effect-fatigue-test.mjs`; `Wever et al., PLOS One 10(10):e0142021 (2015), PMC4651366 (open access)`; `ischemic-conditioning strategy comparisons (e.g. Sci Rep 2025, s41598-025-17442-y, open access)`; `representative open-access cardioprotection study (e.g. Front. Cardiovasc. Med. 2024, PMC10978780)`; `Prichard et al., Tamm review meta-analysis, Forest Ecology and Management (2024); USDA FS TreeSearch 67659 (open)`; `Prichard et al. 2024 (Tamm review)`; `Saunders, Driskell, Johnston & Salas, J. Occup. Health Psychol. 1(2):170-186 (1996); meta-analysis (open via DTIC ADA309082)`.

## Scorecard

- domains (no connecting established theory): materials (metal fatigue) (4/4); biology (cardiac ischemia) (3/3); ecology (wildfire / land systems) (2/2); psychology (behavioral resilience) (1/1)
- total: **10/10** entries confirm the directional prediction
- order-independent null rejected in: **materials (metal fatigue) and biology (cardiac ischemia) and ecology (wildfire / land systems) and psychology (behavioral resilience)**

## Reading

- One pre-registered directional law is corroborated in 10/10 entries spanning 4 domains -- metal fatigue, cardiac ischemia, wildfire ecology, and behavioral resilience -- that share no connecting established theory, have different underlying mechanisms (microstructural fatigue; cellular signalling/chaperones; landscape fuel depletion; psychological coping-skill acquisition), and even different endpoint TYPES (cycles-to-failure; tissue infarct; burn severity; anxiety/performance). The order-INDEPENDENT null (no order effect) is rejected in all 4.
- This is the unification thesis's one no-established-competitor space: no domain-specific theory predicts that fatigue coaxing, ischemic preconditioning, prescribed-fire severity reduction, and stress inoculation obey the same directional order law, yet the grammar does, and the data agree -- including a recurring requirement that the gentle/mild step come FIRST (and proximately).
- HONEST LIMITS: directional cross-domain REACH for the lens standing, NOT decisive. (1) "Mild-first protects" is a very general directional pattern; a skeptic can say any system with memory, adaptation, or resource depletion shows it -- and the four domains here have DIFFERENT mechanisms and even different endpoint types, so this is explicitly a claim about one shared DIRECTION, not one shared mechanism or substrate. (2) Magnitude is a lens, not a quantitative prediction (established per-domain models win there). (3) The mechanistic and endpoint disanalogies (esp. fire = fuel depletion not memory; psychology = function not survival) are the point, not a flaw: the grammar is a directional organiser across unrelated systems.
- CONFIDENCE: unchanged (5.6 / 5.2 / 4.8; cap 6.25). The ledger strengthens the "organises and directionally predicts across domains" claim of the lens framing; it does not raise the metrics.
